extraction.one_line_summary: BENNETT Sam won the sprint but was relegated for shouldering, handing ACKERMANN Pascal his first stage win after appearing poorly positioned 200m out.
tactical_patterns: BENNETT overcame catastrophically deep positioning at 200m to go, proving late momentum trumps early wheel choice in Spanish finishing circuits.
MØRKØV's leadout timing created gap that dissolved when BENNETT forced physical contact, revealing desperation outweighs positioning advantage.
UAE deployed dual threat with PHILIPSEN but BORA's single-option discipline kept ACKERMANN sheltered despite apparent numerical disadvantage.
SÁEZ crashed mid-stage destroying helmet but regained peloton, suggesting early-stage attrition creates no selection on flat Vuelta stages.

rider_signals.BENNETT Sam: negative: demonstrated superior closing speed but tactical immaturity cost stage via relegation for excessive shouldering
rider_signals.ACKERMANN Pascal: positive: won via relegation but positioning at 200m suggests he was already losing the sprint on merit
rider_signals.MØRKØV Michael: positive: executed textbook leadout that delivered BENNETT to victory before relegation, confirms elite piloting skills
rider_signals.PHILIPSEN Jasper: neutral: UAE dual-threat strategy failed to fracture BORA train, suggesting numerical advantage means little without positioning
rider_signals.SÁEZ Héctor: neutral: recovered from helmet-destroying crash to rejoin peloton, proving flat stages offer realistic regrouping windows
